---
title: "Post-Estimation Workflows in bsvarPost"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Post-Estimation Workflows in bsvarPost}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>", eval = FALSE)
```

```{r setup, eval = TRUE, include = FALSE}
library(bsvarPost)
post     <- readRDS("fixtures/fiscal_post_bsvar.rds")
post_alt <- readRDS("fixtures/fiscal_post_bsvar_alt.rds")
```

This vignette picks up where "Getting Started" left off. After extracting impulse
responses and CDMs, deeper analytical questions remain: Which draw best represents
the posterior as a whole? When exactly does a spending shock peak, and how long
does it last? Which structural shocks drove observed fiscal dynamics? This vignette
works through those questions using the same `us_fiscal_lsuw` dataset — quarterly
US data on tax revenue (`ttr`), government spending (`gs`), and output (`gdp`) —
comparing a one-lag baseline against a three-lag alternative specification.

## Representative-model summaries

Pointwise posterior medians may not correspond to any one coherent structural
draw. `median_target_irf()` finds the stored draw whose IRF matrix is closest
(in L2 distance) to the posterior median. The result is a single self-consistent
model that typifies the posterior center.

```{r, eval = TRUE}
rep_irf <- median_target_irf(post, horizon = 20)
summary(rep_irf)
```

The `draw_index` slot records which posterior draw was selected. This can be used
to extract other quantities (CDMs, FEVD) from the same draw for a fully coherent
representative model.

A pre-rendered figure from the same posterior at the full S = 200 resolution:

```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/representative-showcase.png")
```

The representative IRF for the `gs` spending shock shows the typical fiscal
transmission shape: an immediate positive effect on government spending itself,
with the `gdp` response peaking a few quarters later.

## Response timing summaries

Researchers often need to report precisely *when* effects arrive and *how long*
they persist. `bsvarPost` provides four timing summaries, each with full
posterior uncertainty.

**Peak response** — at what horizon does the fiscal spending shock produce the
largest `gdp` effect?

```{r, eval = TRUE}
peak_response(post, type = "irf", horizon = 20, variables = 3, shocks = 2)
```

**Duration response** — how many quarters does the cumulative multiplier remain
positive?

```{r, eval = TRUE}
duration_response(
  post,
  type     = "cdm",
  horizon  = 20,
  variables = 3,
  shocks   = 2,
  relation = ">",
  value    = 0,
  mode     = "total"
)
```

**Half-life** — how quickly does the impulse response decay from its peak?

```{r, eval = TRUE}
half_life_response(
  post,
  type     = "irf",
  horizon  = 20,
  variables = 3,
  shocks   = 2,
  baseline = "peak"
)
```

**Time to threshold** — when does the cumulative multiplier first exceed zero?

```{r, eval = TRUE}
time_to_threshold(
  post,
  type     = "cdm",
  horizon  = 20,
  variables = 3,
  shocks   = 2,
  relation = ">",
  value    = 0
)
```

These four summaries characterise when the fiscal spending effect arrives, how
large it becomes, and how quickly it dissipates — the standard set of timing
statistics for fiscal multiplier papers.

## Comparing response summaries

Does the estimated timing change under a three-lag specification? The
`compare_*` helpers place both posteriors side by side.

```{r, eval = TRUE}
cmp_peak <- compare_peak_response(
  baseline    = post,
  alternative = post_alt,
  type        = "irf",
  horizon     = 20,
  variables   = 3,
  shocks      = 2
)
cmp_peak
```

```{r, eval = TRUE}
compare_duration_response(
  baseline    = post,
  alternative = post_alt,
  type        = "cdm",
  horizon     = 20,
  variables   = 3,
  shocks      = 2,
  relation    = ">",
  value       = 0
)
```

`as_kable()` formats any `bsvar_post_tbl` as a compact `knitr::kable` table
ready for Rmd reports.

```{r, eval = TRUE}
as_kable(cmp_peak, preset = "compact")
```

The three-lag alternative typically shows a later peak with wider credible bands,
reflecting the richer lag dynamics.

## Historical decomposition events

Historical decompositions (HD) measure each structural shock's contribution to
observed variation in each variable at each point in time.

For full-sample interpretation, start with `tidy_hd()` and one of the dedicated
plot helpers. `plot_hd_stacked()` gives the standard composition view,
`plot_hd_overlay()` compares shock paths within each variable, and
`plot_hd_total()` overlays the reconstructed series on the observed path.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
hd_full <- tidy_hd(post)
plot_hd_stacked(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3)
plot_hd_overlay(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3)
plot_hd_total(post, variables = "gdp", shocks = c("gs", "ttr"))
```

`tidy_hd_event()` then aggregates those contributions over a chosen event
window.

The `us_fiscal_lsuw` sample begins in 1948. To examine which shocks drove fiscal
dynamics in the first year (four quarters: 1948.25 through 1948.75):

```{r, eval = TRUE}
hd_event <- tidy_hd_event(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75")
head(hd_event)
```

`shock_ranking()` ranks the structural shocks by their absolute contribution to a
target variable over the same window. Which shock moved `gdp` the most?

```{r, eval = TRUE}
shock_ranking(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75", ranking = "absolute")
```

For a composition-oriented event view, `plot_hd_event_share()` rescales the
same event-window contributions into shares:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
plot_hd_event_share(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75", top_n = 3)
```

A pre-rendered HD event figure from a richer S = 200 run:

```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/hd-event-showcase.png")
```

## Publication-ready reporting

All `bsvarPost` outputs are `bsvar_post_tbl` data frames and accept the same
family of reporting helpers.

**knitr table** (always available):

```{r, eval = TRUE}
as_kable(summary(rep_irf), preset = "compact", digits = 3)
```

**gt table** (when the `gt` package is installed):

```{r, eval = requireNamespace("gt", quietly = TRUE)}
as_gt(cmp_peak, caption = "Peak response: 1-lag vs 3-lag", digits = 3,
      preset = "compact")
```

**flextable** (when the `flextable` package is installed):

```{r, eval = requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)}
as_flextable(cmp_peak, caption = "Peak response: 1-lag vs 3-lag", digits = 3,
             preset = "compact")
```

**Publication plot** — `publish_bsvar_plot()` applies a paper-ready theme and
returns the plot object for further adjustment or direct saving.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
publish_bsvar_plot(rep_irf, preset = "paper")
```

A pre-rendered diagnostics figure demonstrating the publication styling layer:

```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/diagnostics-showcase.png")
```

## Next steps

The **Hypothesis Testing vignette** covers formal posterior probability
statements — `hypothesis_irf()`, `joint_hypothesis_irf()`, and
`simultaneous_irf()` — for testing sign and magnitude restrictions on the
fiscal transmission mechanism.
